topics.summary: A regional war triggered by coordinated U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iranian military infrastructure and leadership, followed by Iranian retaliatory attacks across the Middle East.
topics.catch_up_summary: On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched coordinated strikes on Iranian military sites, leadership, and nuclear-related facilities. Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed; his son Mojtaba was named successor. Iran retaliated with hundreds of missiles and drones targeting U.S. bases in Qatar, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia, and struck Israeli cities. Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz on March 1, triggering an energy crisis with oil prices approaching $120 per barrel. At least 7 U.S. service members and over 1,000 people regionally have been reported killed. Hezbollah opened a second front with rocket fire into Israel on March 5. The conflict has disrupted global aviation, shipping, and energy markets. Diplomatic efforts have stalled after indirect talks in Muscat collapsed in early February.
topics.background: Tensions between the U.S. and Iran escalated following the 2018 U.S. withdrawal from the JCPOA nuclear deal. Intelligence reports of Iranian nuclear program reconstitution, the October 2025 JCPOA snapback sanctions, and the collapse of Muscat talks in February 2026 preceded the military operation. The conflict represents the most significant direct U.S.-Iran engagement since 1988.
why_it_matters: Global oil prices directly affected — Strait of Hormuz closed
Canadian travelers and military personnel potentially impacted
Risk of wider regional war involving Gulf states, Lebanon, and proxies
watchpoints: Any ceasefire or mediation proposal from Gulf states or China

uncertainties: Exact damage to Iranian nuclear facilities

Duration of Strait of Hormuz closure
Possibility of U.S. ground engagement
Cohesion of Iranian state under IRGC-led triumvirate

topics.summary: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine continues into its fifth year with grinding frontline fighting, long-range strikes, and no ceasefire in sight.
topics.catch_up_summary: Russia invaded Ukraine on February 24, 2022. After failing to capture Kyiv, the war settled into prolonged fighting in eastern and southern Ukraine. Russia controls roughly 20% of Ukrainian territory. Ukraine launched counteroffensives reclaiming Kharkiv and Kherson in 2022, and crossed into Russia's Kursk region in 2024. By early 2026, monthly territorial changes are small. Ukraine has lost 70% of its power generation capacity to Russian strikes. A February 2026 Starlink disruption enabled Ukrainian counterattacks reclaiming 33 square miles. Major prisoner exchanges continue. The U.S.-Iran war has diverted international attention and postponed peace talks. Military casualties are catastrophic on both sides.
topics.background: Russia annexed Crimea in 2014 and backed separatists in eastern Ukraine. The 2022 full-scale invasion was the largest military operation in Europe since WWII. Western nations have provided tens of billions in military aid. Diplomatic negotiations have stalled repeatedly.

what_changed_today: Drone attacks resulting in civilian casualties exemplify the ongoing violence between Ukraine and Russia, complicating fragile ceasefire discussions.

topics.summary: U.S. tariff policy is in flux after the Supreme Court struck down most IEEPA tariffs in February 2026, replaced by a temporary 10% global tariff under Section 122.
topics.catch_up_summary: The Trump administration imposed sweeping tariffs starting January 2025, hitting Canada, Mexico, and China hardest. Average effective tariff rates peaked at 27% in April 2025. On February 20, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled IEEPA-based tariffs illegal, creating a $130 billion refund liability. The administration immediately imposed a new 10% global tariff under Section 122 (effective February 24), valid for 150 days. Twenty-four states are suing to block the new tariffs. The tariffs have added an estimated 0.5 percentage points to core inflation and reduced annual household income by roughly $2,100. Canada-U.S. trade relations remain strained with retaliatory measures in place.
topics.background: U.S. protectionist trade policy expanded significantly under the second Trump administration, using emergency powers to impose broad tariffs. The Supreme Court ruling created unprecedented legal uncertainty. A USMCA review is scheduled for mid-2026.
why_it_matters: Direct impact on Canadian exporters and Bay of Quinte manufacturers
Consumer prices rising from tariff pass-through
Canada-U.S. trade relationship under unprecedented legal and economic strain
watchpoints: Whether Section 122 tariffs extend beyond 150 days
USMCA review launch in mid-2026

Impact on March jobs and inflation data
Threatened 200% tariff on French alcohol
uncertainties: Treasury's plan for $130B IEEPA refund without budget destabilization

topics.summary: A stagflationary shock combining weakening labor markets, elevated inflation from tariffs, and an energy crisis from the Iran conflict.
topics.catch_up_summary: The global economy faces a stagflationary shock in early 2026. U.S. nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 in February (unemployment 4.4%). Oil prices surged toward $120/barrel after Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz. Global core inflation is 2.8% but U.S. core CPI remains at 3.2% due to tariff pass-through. The IMF projects 3.3% global growth. Stock markets are volatile — the Nikkei fell 5.2% and Kospi 6% on the energy spike. The Federal Reserve is holding rates while the ECB and Bank of England move toward easing. Tariffs have added 0.5 points to U.S. inflation. Economists warn the oil shock could add 40 basis points to global inflation for every 10% price increase.
topics.background: Post-pandemic inflation surged in 2022, prompting aggressive rate hikes. By 2026, new supply shocks from tariffs and the Iran conflict threaten to reignite inflation just as labor markets weaken.
why_it_matters: Mortgage rates, food prices, and cost of living for Canadian families
Job market stability threatened by combined shocks
Bay of Quinte manufacturing and agriculture affected

uncertainties: Duration of Iran-related oil shock

topics.summary: The Israel-Hamas conflict is in a fragile ceasefire phase since October 2025, with over 74,000 dead, humanitarian access crippled, and the situation now intertwined with the Iran conflict.
topics.catch_up_summary: Hamas attacked southern Israel on October 7, 2023, killing approximately 1,200 and taking 251 hostages. Israel's military response has killed over 72,000 Palestinians (Gaza Health Ministry) and displaced 90% of Gaza's population. After multiple rounds of fighting and truces, a comprehensive ceasefire was reached in October 2025. However, violations continue — Israel launched airstrikes killing 32 in January 2026 citing violations. On March 1, 2026, Israel banned 37 humanitarian agencies including UNRWA from Gaza. A U.S.-supervised 'Cairo Committee' for Gaza governance held its first meeting in January 2026. The situation has merged with the broader Iran conflict as Hezbollah resumed rocket fire into Israel.
topics.background: The Israeli-Palestinian conflict spans decades. The October 7 attack was the deadliest day in Israeli history. The resulting military operation and humanitarian crisis drew international legal scrutiny including ICJ proceedings.
why_it_matters: Massive humanitarian crisis with 1.9 million displaced
Canadian policy positions on Middle East affected

uncertainties: Whether ceasefire survives the broader Iran conflict

topics.summary: Sudan's civil war has passed 1,000 days with the SAF recapturing Khartoum but the RSF controlling Darfur. Over 150,000 dead, 12.3 million displaced, and 25 million facing severe hunger.
topics.catch_up_summary: Sudan's civil war between the SAF and RSF began April 15, 2023 and has passed 1,000 days. The SAF recaptured Khartoum and Omdurman in March 2025 but the RSF seized El Fasher in October 2025 and controls most of Darfur. The RSF has established a parallel government. Death toll estimates exceed 150,000. Over 12.3 million are displaced (world's largest crisis) and 25 million face severe food insecurity including 4 million malnourished children. The current phase centers on the SAF's Kordofan offensive — recapturing Bara on March 5 and breaking the Kadugli siege. Chad closed its border after RSF drone strikes killed Chadian soldiers. The U.S. sanctioned RSF commanders in February 2026.
topics.background: The conflict emerged from a power struggle between military leaders who jointly overthrew Sudan's civilian government in 2021. South Sudan faces spillover instability with oil exports vulnerable to northern fighting.
why_it_matters: World's largest displacement crisis — 12.3 million displaced
25 million facing severe hunger including famine conditions
